description: During the 1980s, the mass adoption of Automated Teller Machines (ATMs) in the US led to widespread fear of teller unemployment. However, data later showed the cost of operating bank branches dropped, leading to more branches and actually more tellers (though in different roles).

key_events.description: Major banks announce aggressive ATM rollouts to cut overhead.
Labor unions voice initial opposition citing job losses.
Economic reports surface showing banks opening more branches to compete for customers.
Employment data shows teller numbers remained stable, shifting consensus toward technological acceptance.

calibration_notes: Useful for modeling scenarios where public opinion shifts from 'fear of loss' to 'acceptance of productivity gains' over time.
